experiments.title: Using an Exit-Intent Popup to Increase Donor Conversion During a High-Urgency Campaign (May Match 2025)
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that presenting a lower donation ask in an exit-intent pop-up will encourage more users to complete their gift, capturing donations that might have otherwise been abandoned during a high-urgency campaign.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/06/2025 - 06/03/2025
As part of the May Match 2025 campaign—an urgency-driven effort to acquire new monthly donors (FamilyLife Partners)—we wanted to test whether an exit intent popup on the donation page could reduce abandonment and increase conversions.
To do this, we decided to present an exit-intent popup, which would give users a lower-barrier monthly donation ask if they showed intent to leave the page, and instead encourage them to complete a smaller gift. The treatment experience included a popup triggered by mouse-exit behavior on desktop or time delay on mobile, offering a $10/month ask with emotionally resonant, impact-focused messaging. The control experience maintained the standard donation page with no popup intervention.
We intentionally excluded visitors who had previously interacted with an impact popup earlier in the session, to isolate the effect of this standalone exit-intent experience. The popup emphasized that even a small monthly gift could make a lasting difference—supporting 76 families with Christ-centered resources.
We wanted to focus this test on recapturing users who demonstrated exit behavior, hypothesizing that a timely, emotionally framed, and low-friction ask could nudge hesitant users to commit before abandoning the donation process.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This test demonstrated that introducing an exit-intent popup with a lower-barrier ask on the donation page can meaningfully reduce abandonment and increase both one-time and recurring donor conversion—especially during high-urgency campaigns like May Match.
The treatment experience, which offered a $10/month giving option when users showed intent to leave, outperformed the control by 44.2% in overall donor conversion (2.7% vs. 1.8%), achieving statistical significance at 99.4% confidence.
Looking at giving types:
Recurring gifts more than doubled, with a 102.1% increase in recurring donor conversion (0.7% vs. 0.3%, 98.3% confidence).One-time gifts also increased by 32.2% (2.1% vs. 1.6%, 94.2% confidence).
These results suggest that a well-timed, emotionally compelling, and low-friction offer can recapture hesitant donors right before they leave—and that framing the donation ask as small but impactful can activate both urgency and achievability.
The success of the recurring donor uplift is particularly important in the context of long-term donor value. This test validates the exit-intent popup as a powerful conversion tool and opens the door for future testing around:
Tailoring the popup message for one-time vs. recurring emphasisOptimizing design and placement for mobile vs. desktopTesting variations in gift amounts and message tone (e.g., more urgent vs. more relational)
This was a high-impact win with broad implications for donation page optimization.

experiments.title: How defaulting to recurring gift on a premium offer donation page impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that defaulting to a recurring gift and emphasizing its broader impact for FamilyLife donors will increase the number of recurring donations because it presents a more manageable commitment and highlights the significance of ongoing support, encouraging more donors to commit to long-term giving.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/14/2025 - 03/14/2025
FamilyLife is a Christian organization focused on strengthening marriages and families by providing resources, events, and guidance rooted in biblical principles, helping people navigate the challenges of family life, and fostering lasting, healthy relationships. In this experiment, we aimed to test whether defaulting to a recurring gift option and emphasizing its impact would increase recurring donations.
The test involved two variations on the donation page. The Control version defaulted to a one-time gift of $50 and included a call-out stating, “Equip 39 families with practical insights to strengthen the relationships that matter most.” Conversely, the Treatment defaulted to a $25 recurring gift and featured a more compelling call-out: “Equip 233 families with practical insights to strengthen the relationships that matter most.” This A/B test was split 50/50 and was tested on Seeing Beautiful Again, one of FamilyLife’s best performing offers.
We hypothesize that by highlighting the broader impact of recurring gifts and making it the default option, we would see an increase in the number of donors choosing to make recurring contributions, ultimately boosting donor retention and long-term support for FamilyLife’s mission.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Through this retest, we observed that defaulting to a recurring gift option once again led to an increase in recurring donations. The Treatment group, which featured recurring giving as the default, generated 16 recurring gifts compared to 0 in the Control group. This resulted in a 0.92% recurring conversion rate, validated by a 99.9% level of confidence—providing strong evidence that this approach effectively encourages more donors to commit to ongoing support.
When looking at immediate revenue, there was little difference between the two groups. The Control group generated $902 in revenue, while the Treatment brought in $1,017—a modest increase that is not statistically significant. However, while short-term revenue remained stable, the shift toward recurring gifts has the potential to yield much greater long-term value.
Although overall transactions were slightly lower in the Treatment group (93 gifts) compared to the Control (105 gifts), this was observed at a 66.8% level of confidence. This outcome is to be expected, as introducing a default recurring gift option may cause some one-time donors to reconsider their gift in the moment. However, those who do proceed are more likely to become committed, long-term supporters.
Next Steps:
We recommend that FamilyLife continue to default to a recurring gift on their donation pages. This should be a permanent update to their microsite.Continuing to test and optimize recurring donation strategies: Based on the success of this experiment, it is essential to explore and refine different approaches to promoting recurring gifts. Testing variations in messaging, default options, and impact statements can help further increase recurring donations.Monitor Long-Term Performance: Track the performance of recurring donors to assess their impact on retention and overall organizational goals.

experiments.title: How defaulting to recurring gift on a premium offer donation page impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that defaulting to a recurring gift and emphasizing its broader impact for FamilyLife donors will increase the number of recurring donations because it presents a more manageable commitment and highlights the significance of ongoing support, encouraging more donors to commit to long-term giving.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/01/2024 - 08/29/2024
FamilyLife is a Christian organization focused on strengthening marriages and families by providing resources, events, and guidance rooted in biblical principles, helping people navigate the challenges of family life, and fostering lasting, healthy relationships. In this experiment, we aimed to test whether defaulting to a recurring gift option and emphasizing its impact would increase recurring donations.
The test involved two variations on the donation page. The Control version defaulted to a one-time gift of $50 and included a call-out stating, “Equip 39 families with practical insights to strengthen the relationships that matter most.” Conversely, the Treatment defaulted to a $15 recurring gift and featured a more compelling call-out: “Equip 140 families with practical insights to strengthen the relationships that matter most.” This A/B test was split 50/50 and was tested on 5 premium donation pages, including two best-performing offers: Seeing Beautiful Again and 52 Weeks in the Word.
We hypothesize that by highlighting the broader impact of recurring gifts and making it the default option, we would see an increase in the number of donors choosing to make recurring contributions, ultimately boosting donor retention and long-term support for FamilyLife’s mission.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Through this experiment, we observed that defaulting to a recurring gift resulted in 20 conversions compared to 0 in the Control group, marking a 0.53% increase in recurring donations with a 100% level of confidence. Revenue was down by 29.4%, but this was at a 16.1% level of confidence, indicating that the change is not statistically significant and suggesting no substantial negative impact on immediate revenue. Overall transactions were lower, reflecting the shift from one-time gifts to recurring donations. However, we identified that FamilyLife's recurring donors have a significantly higher retention rate of 77% compared to 24% for episodic donors, an increase in retention of 247%, underscoring the value of focusing on recurring contributions for the organization's long-term sustainability.
The increase in recurring donations can be attributed to the effective use of a default recurring gift option, which likely made it easier for donors to commit to ongoing support. The enhanced messaging, which emphasized the broader impact of recurring gifts, probably resonated more with donors and encouraged them to choose the recurring option.
Next Steps:
We recommend that FamilyLife continue to default to a recurring gift on their donation pages. This should be a permanent update to their microsite.Refine Impact Messaging: Continue testing variations of impact messaging to determine the most effective approach for driving recurring donations.Optimize Recurring Amounts: Experiment with different recurring gift amounts to find the most appealing option for donors.Monitor Long-Term Performance: Track the performance of recurring donors to assess their impact on retention and overall organizational goals.

experiments.title: How strengthening the value proposition of a daily devotional subscription impacts email conversion on the landing page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe strengthening the appeal and exclusivity of a daily devotional subscription will increase the email conversion on its landing page
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/31/2023 - 08/07/2023
For Bible League Canada’s Words from the Heart daily devotional, we felt the Value Proposition was weaker than it should be. Particularly in the exclusivity and appeal it conveyed. The control page seemed to assume that visitors would already believe that they should be reading their Bibles each day, instead of reminding them why daily Bible reading may be important.
We hypothesized that the landing page’s value proposition could be strengthened by first articulating why a daily devotional is important and then explaining how this short, exclusive devotional would enable subscribers to experience the peace and presence of Christ. To do this, we added a scripture reference that encouraged believers to daily meditate on God’s Word. Then, we emphasized the negative things that can happen when believers don’t take time to follow this verse’s instruction and meditate on Scripture. Finally, we emphasized three separate times in the copy that this was an “exclusive 3-minute daily devotional.”
experiments.keyLearnings: After just one full week, we were able to validate the impact of this strengthened value proposition. This test was a reminder that we shouldn't assume that our audience already understands the full value of an offer. Instead, we should take time to reinforce the need the offer meets as well as the exclusive ways it will benefit users. We will continue to test the value proposition on our other landing pages to ensure that we are providing greater appeal and exclusivity to visitors.

experiments.title: How a premium offer chat widget CTA on podcast pages impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a chat message widget to the podcast pages for visitors of FamilyLife Today® will create a more engaging and persuasive experience due to the interactive nature of the widget, therefore increasing donor conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/02/2024 - 07/31/2024
FamilyLife helps families bridge the gap between their intentions and reality, fostering closer relationships with God, each other, and their communities. Their FamilyLife Podcast Network, including the FamilyLife Today® podcast, offers engaging conversations to help families grow together with Jesus and strengthen their most important relationships.
Traditionally, FamilyLife has relied on an end-of-article “dear listener” message to promote their campaigns and offer a premium item to donors. We hypothesized that adding a chat message widget to the podcast pages for listeners and visitors of FamilyLife Today® will increase donor conversion rates because the interactive and personal nature of the widget creates a more engaging and persuasive experience through social presence and personal connection.
For this test, we selected their most popular premium offer, the book “Seeing Beautiful Again.” The Control was the traditional “dear listener” message, while the Treatment was a chat message widget. Traffic was split evenly between the two versions, with identical language used in both. The Control message appeared at the end of the article, while the widget popped up in the bottom right corner and expanded when clicked. The Treatment also included images of the FamilyLife Today podcast hosts, Dave and Ann, to simulate a text messaging experience.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running this experiment for 29 days, we found that the chat message widget outperformed the traditional "dear listener" approach, achieving a conversion rate of 0.37% compared to 0.13% for the control. This represents a 187.9% increase in conversions with a high level of confidence at 98.8%.The results demonstrate that the interactive nature of the widget significantly enhanced donor engagement and conversion rates.
For future experimentation, we plan to test this approach during the Fiscal Year End (FYE) Campaign using a premium book offer, "Unoffendable" by Brant Hansen. We also aim to explore different design variations of the chat widget, evaluate other premium offers for their effectiveness with interactive features, and assess the widget’s performance across various sections of the website.
Overall, the key learnings from this experiment emphasize the importance of personalization, interactivity, and social presence in driving donor conversions. By leveraging these insights in future experimentation, FamilyLife can continue to optimize its donation strategies and create more impactful and engaging experiences for its audience.

experiments.title: How authentic and impact-driven language on a premium offer donation page impacted donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a testimonial and increasing the authenticity and value impact language for people on the premium offer donation page will achieve an increase in donor conversion because the increased value prop language will give readers more clarity into what the premium offer is about and how it will add value to their lives. because  the increased value prop language will give readers more clarity into what the premium offer is about and how it will add value to their lives.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 08/25/2023
FamilyLife is a Christian ministry organization that creates and shares resources to help families grow the relationships that matter most. One of their best-performing premium offers is a book called Seeing Beautiful Again, and we decided to test new value prop language on this offer’s donation page to see if we could improve the donor conversion even more.
For the treatment version of this page, we wanted readers to understand what the Seeing Beautiful Again book is about and how the lessons of the book can impact their own lives. To do this, we added the following to the body copy on this page:
Increased clarity in the body copy to explain the purpose of the book and the problems it will help the reader solve
Bullet points with clear impact statements to tell readers what specific lessons/tools the book will give them
An emotional and authentic review from someone who read the book to provide social proof
Our hypothesis was that the social proof and  increased clarity would lead to a higher rate of donor donor conversion on the treatment version of the page. We created the new treatment and split the traffic evenly between the control and treatment. Since we were running acquisition ads for this offer, we knew we’d have enough traffic to validate the impact of the new disclaimer.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a testimonial and increasing the authenticity and value impact language on the premium offer donation page significantly increased donor conversion. The treatment version of the page resulted in a 954.4% increase in donations with a 99.9% statistical level of confidence.
Based on this result, future experimentation should focus on incorporating social proof and increasing the clarity of the value proposition on donation pages. Testimonials from people who have been impacted by FamilyLife's content offers or premium offers can be used to help build trust and credibility, while clear language that explains the purpose of the offer and the problems it solves can help readers better understand the value they will receive.
Our next steps will focus on testing testimonials and impact language on FamilyLife's other offers to see if we can achieve this lift in donor conversion with other offers.

experiments.title: How a homepage donation modal impacts donor conversion during a high urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a homepage donation modal will increase donor conversion by reducing friction and increasing the appeal of the donor CTA
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/08/2023 - 05/20/2023
During FamilyLife’s May Recurring Campaign, we wanted to increase donor conversion on the homepage. They were offering both a premium and a matching challenge for new recurring donors. We hypothesized that featuring a “modal” in the center of the page with the image of the premium offer and copy highlighting the matching fund, we could increase the appeal of the CTA and reduce friction by allowing donors to begin their gift right on the homepage.
We split the traffic between our standard homepage during the campaign and the treatment with the mid-page modal.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the homepage donation modal did not result in an increase in donor conversion. In fact, it led to a significant decrease in donations across all traffic sources.
This suggests that the homepage donation modal created friction or detracted from the user experience, leading to a decrease in donor conversion. It is possible that the modal was seen as intrusive or distracting, causing visitors to abandon the donation process. Also, the messaging of the modal may not have been aligned with the motivation of the traffic to the homepage. More testing would be needed to see if a different premium offer or value proposition angle would lead to higher conversion.

experiments.title: How an end of article dear reader emphasizing how few readers donate impacts donor conversion during a high urgency campaign

rows.name: End of Article Dear Reader - premium focused
End of Article Dear Reader - premium with 3% language

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a dear reader message that emphasizes how few readers donate will increase donor conversion during a high urgency campaign
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/01/2023 - 05/20/2023
During FamilyLife’s annual Spring recurring campaign, we wanted to test the best placement for a dear reader message featuring copy that mentions less than 3% of articles readers give each month to keep this content here for them on-air and online.
Traditionally, they’ve relied on an end-of-article dear reader promoting the campaign and offering a premium offer to those who donate. We wanted to test this against a dear reader that added language about how less than 3% of readers donate, believing this would increase the urgency of the CTA. To validate the placement of the message, we would test that same 3% language in a dynamic dear reader that would appear at the top of the page and become a sticky bar on scrolling. Due to size constraints of this placement, we would remove the language about the premium.
We split their article traffic evenly between all three versions of the page and measured the impact on recurring donations.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We found that emphasizing how few readers donate was compelling, no matter the placement. The best version of the dear reader in the experiment was the one that emphasized how few donate and offered the premium at the end of the article. More testing is needed to see if the above article deployment would have performed better if it too had offered the premium. Due to size constraints of that deployment, we did not get test that this time around.
Going forward we will continue to use language about how few readers donate in our dear reader messages, with additional testing focusing on ways to further refine and optimize that message.

experiments.title: How Adding a “Start My Gift” Button to the Top of a Year-End Donation Page impacts donor conversion for mobile traffic
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a “start my gift” button at the top of the year-end donation page will increase donor conversion by decreasing donor friction
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/02/2022 - 12/18/2022
FamilyLife is a Christian ministry serving families worldwide. For their calendar year-end campaign, they wanted to test ways of improving donor conversion, particularly for mobile traffic which has typically had a much lower conversion rate on their site. We hypothesized that the length of the copy on the page might be adding friction to motivated traffic and wanted to see if we could reduce that friction by providing a button that would invite visitors to “start my gift” right at the top of the page. Clicking the button would drop the visitor directly to the donation form.
We ran the experiment on their campaign donation page for almost two weeks.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While we did not achieve a greater than 95% level of confidence for all traffic, mobile validated a lift and all other primary channels showed a strong positive impact with a directional level of confidence. What was surprising was that this button also increased revenue. We excluded any gifts over $750 that would skew the results and still achieved a 237% increase in revenue from the treatment with a 94% level of confidence. We will need to do additional testing to dig into why this impacted revenue so strongly but we will use this button for the remainder of the campaign.

Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:

experiments.title: How matching gift copy affects clickthrough rate on a modal popup
experiments.researchQuestion: How does matching gift language affect clickthrough rate on a modal popup window?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/26/2018 - 12/28/2017
FamilyLife was in the final week of their year-end campaign, and launched a modal popup to point web traffic to the donation page. The popup contained a countdown clock as well as urgency-focused message. The default version of the popup had language that echoed the headline, but they wondered if including matching gift language would increase clickthrough rate.
They created a treatment that kept urgent language, but added in the fact that the gift would be matched dollar-for-dollar. They launched a test to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment (with matching gift language) produced a 56% increase in clickthrough rate. This test revealed that while urgency is key to any message with a deadline (especially at the end of the year), incentive can provide additional lift for action oriented copy.
In the control, the potential donor is presented with four elements to inspire them to click through and give: an urgent headline, a countdown clock, additional copy above the button, and the button copy. But in the treatment, the matching gift language provides a fifth element, without leaving out any of the previous four—incentive.
Many times, calls-to-action have one of these elements, but not the other. This test shows the power of combining the two.

experiments.title: How a radical redesign of an email appeal and donation page affected donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: How will a radical redesign of both the email and the landing page impact donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/14/2017 - 07/21/2017
The treatment donation flow produced a statistically valid 272.1% increase in donor conversion. This strong leap forward was due to a few identifiable conversion factors:
Reduction of visual friction in the control email led to more readers engaging with the copy.
Personal tone in the email increased motivation to give.
Decreased friction on the landing page increased likelihood to complete a donation
However, since all of these optimizations were made in one fell swoop, we cannot know exactly how each change contributed to the total increase. That said, this experiment provides a new benchmark for email appeal testing for FamilyLife—one that can be optimized in its own right.
experiments.keyLearnings:
FamilyLife had a mature email fundraising program that relied heavily on third-party stories with large images. wanted to test an entirely new approach to their email fundraising that integrated a more personal tone and removed possible sources of friction from the “mental conversation” provoked by the email.
The control email used a template with a large FamilyLife logo at the top, followed by an image, a pull quote and a donate button, before any actual email copy. The email copy made an offer to provide a scholarship for a couple like “Fred and Melissa”, even though that couple had not been introduced yet. This presented clarity issues—who are Fred and Melissa? The couple in the picture? The call to action never really requested a gift, it just suggested a gift by linking text telling the reader what their gift would do. Finally, the email had a donate button at the bottom as well. This email led to a landing page that had the headline “Scholarships for Hardworking Pastor Couples”, which didn’t address the reader. That copy gave more information about Fred and Melissa, and then led into a separate call to action section (visually separated by a green box) that was disconnected from the pastor-centric message: “Help Families Stay Together”. This extra section then led to a donation form that had all the fields stacked on top of each other.
The FamilyLife team started with the email, developing a treatment that stripped out all the visual elements to reduce visual friction and resemble an email that their founder, Dennis Rainey, might actually write. Then they took a new approach with the tone, making it very personal. The treatment copy talked about his wife, their history serving pastors, and the challenges they’ve seen firsthand. The ask was vey clear and direct to the reader, and provided just a single link to the donation page, with no button.
This led to a donation page that led with a headline that extends an offer to the reader: Invest in God’s work by strengthening a pastor’s marriage. This copy removed the story, with the hypothesis that the third-party story with no real connection and anonymized names might detract from the clarity of the value proposition. The treatment added a bulleted list of what the pastor couple would receive at a Weekend to Remember event, and made a clear ask to the reader. The visual separation from the green box was removed, and the donation form was shortened by bringing multiple fields (i.e. first name and last name) onto the same line, which gave the perception of less friction, while still collecting the same information.
They split their file evenly (making sure to evenly split donors and non-donors) and sent one version to each segment. The control email linked to the control page and the treatment email to the treatment page to preserve the integrity of the test throughout the entire appeal flow.

experiments.title: How a radical redesign of an email appeal and donation page affected donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: How will a radical redesign of both the email and the landing page impact donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/14/2017 - 07/21/2017
The treatment donation flow produced a statistically valid 272.1% increase in donor conversion. This strong leap forward was due to a few identifiable conversion factors:
Reduction of visual friction in the control email led to more readers engaging with the copy.
Personal tone in the email increased motivation to give.
Decreased friction on the landing page increased likelihood to complete a donation
However, since all of these optimizations were made in one fell swoop, we cannot know exactly how each change contributed to the total increase. That said, this experiment provides a new benchmark for email appeal testing for FamilyLife—one that can be optimized in its own right.
experiments.keyLearnings:
FamilyLife had a mature email fundraising program that relied heavily on third-party stories with large images. wanted to test an entirely new approach to their email fundraising that integrated a more personal tone and removed possible sources of friction from the “mental conversation” provoked by the email.

The control email used a template with a large FamilyLife logo at the top, followed by an image, a pull quote and a donate button, before any actual email copy. The email copy made an offer to provide a scholarship for a couple like “Fred and Melissa”, even though that couple had not been introduced yet. This presented clarity issues—who are Fred and Melissa? The couple in the picture? The call to action never really requested a gift, it just suggested a gift by linking text telling the reader what their gift would do. Finally, the email had a donate button at the bottom as well. This email led to a landing page that had the headline “Scholarships for Hardworking Pastor Couples”, which didn’t address the reader. That copy gave more information about Fred and Melissa, and then led into a separate call to action section (visually separated by a green box) that was disconnected from the pastor-centric message: “Help Families Stay Together”. This extra section then led to a donation form that had all the fields stacked on top of each other.

The FamilyLife team started with the email, developing a treatment that stripped out all the visual elements to reduce visual friction and resemble an email that their founder, Dennis Rainey, might actually write. Then they took a new approach with the tone, making it very personal. The treatment copy talked about his wife, their history serving pastors, and the challenges they’ve seen firsthand. The ask was vey clear and direct to the reader, and provided just a single link to the donation page, with no button.

This led to a donation page that led with a headline that extends an offer to the reader: Invest in God’s work by strengthening a pastor’s marriage. This copy removed the story, with the hypothesis that the third-party story with no real connection and anonymized names might detract from the clarity of the value proposition. The treatment added a bulleted list of what the pastor couple would receive at a Weekend to Remember event, and made a clear ask to the reader. The visual separation from the green box was removed, and the donation form was shortened by bringing multiple fields (i.e. first name and last name) onto the same line, which gave the perception of less friction, while still collecting the same information.

They split their file evenly (making sure to evenly split donors and non-donors) and sent one version to each segment. The control email linked to the control page and the treatment email to the treatment page to preserve the integrity of the test throughout the entire appeal flow.

experiments.title: How using a multi-offer campaign in Facebook affects click through rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Combining offers into one campaign will reduce cost per click and cost per donor acquired.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/15/2021 - 05/01/2021
The FamilyLife resources team asked us to take on their Facebook marketing and strategy. The first thing we wanted to test was how a multi-offer campaign that combined all of their offers into one campaign performed against campaigns that included one offer each. We tested the multi-offer campaign with four offers in it, against the four offers in individual campaigns. This test was set up as a test and learn in Facebook and ran for 6 weeks. The four campaigns had an equal combined spend with the multi-offer campaign.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The multi-offer campaign did increase clicks, and so increased traffic to their site, by 62%. That in turn reduced the cost per click from $1.80 to $1.30, a decrease of 28%. We hypothesis that this happened because instead of requiring Facebook to use the budgets to compete for spend in the different audiences, it allowed Facebook to serve the best offer to the best candidate in those different audiences and so allowed the Facebook algorithm to do it's best work. That meant it had to spend less to reach the best lead.

In addition to decreasing the cost per click and increasing the click through rate, this test also increased the number of purchases by 59% and decreased the cost per acquisition by 26% taking it from $120 CPA to $89 CPA. (This data is according to the Facebook 7-day attribution window. We did not have the capability at this time to confirm this data through Google Analytics tracking.) The same hypothesis applies here. We allowed the algorithm to find the best leads through this campaign set up.

While this approach proved to resonate with new course takers, we hypothesized that a smaller, more impactful figure might increase motivation even more. We developed a treatment that instead of communicating how many people $50 could reach, it communicated how many more couples $50 would allow to sign-up for the online course. experiments.keyLearnings: By telling people their specific gift of $50 would allow 36 more couple to sign up for the online course significantly impacted conversion by 98%! The smaller amount (36 couples vs 7,000 people) communicated a greater impact. This greater impact resonated with new course takers and increased their motivation to give. Allowing more couples to take the course is something that has a stronger personal connection to the end user and is easier to consume and believe.

FamilyLife offers a free online course called, I Still Do. After a person signs up for the online course, they are presented with an instant donation ask. After reading the copy on the instant donation page, we felt that there were two competing messages; one – about the details of the online course, and two – asking people to make a donation. We hypothesized that we might be able to increase donor conversion if we focused the value proposition solely on the primary goal of the page – the donation ask. To do this, we stripped out all of the details regarding the course and started our messaging around the donation. To reinforce the value proposition of the ask, we also told people the number of people they could reach with a specific, suggested, dollar amount right before the call-to-action.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment donation page increased donor conversion significantly by 165.8%! We were able to significantly increase donor motivation by strengthening the value proposition. This was done by simply focusing the content of the page on ONE primary message - the donation ask. 

It is important to understand what the purpose of your page is for. In this case, we were trying to communicate too many messages to the end user which resulted in a low conversion rate for the control. When we were able to re-focus the page around what we want people to do, our messaging was much stronger and our case for support became much more clear.

In both instances, the person was taken to a page to complete their registration once they clicked the button. Design elements remained the same.
experiments.keyLearnings: Offering the online course in the header increased acquisition by 47%. By offering the online course, we not only offered people something with a higher perceived value, but the copy brought clarity to what the offer was. These two factors increased the appeal of the offer and as a result more people were motivated to convert and get the offer.

experiments.title: How a radical redesign of an email appeal and donation page affected donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: How will a radical redesign of both the email and the landing page impact donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/14/2017 - 07/21/2017
FamilyLife had a mature email fundraising program that relied heavily on third-party stories with large images. wanted to test an entirely new approach to their email fundraising that integrated a more personal tone and removed possible sources of friction from the “mental conversation” provoked by the email.
The control email used a template with a large FamilyLife logo at the top, followed by an image, a pull quote and a donate button, before any actual email copy. The email copy made an offer to provide a scholarship for a couple like “Fred and Melissa”, even though that couple had not been introduced yet. This presented clarity issues—who are Fred and Melissa? The couple in the picture? The call to action never really requested a gift, it just suggested a gift by linking text telling the reader what their gift would do. Finally, the email had a donate button at the bottom as well. This email led to a landing page that had the headline “Scholarships for Hardworking Pastor Couples”, which didn’t address the reader. That copy gave more information about Fred and Melissa, and then led into a separate call to action section (visually separated by a green box) that was disconnected from the pastor-centric message: “Help Families Stay Together”. This extra section then led to a donation form that had all the fields stacked on top of each other.
The FamilyLife team started with the email, developing a treatment that stripped out all the visual elements to reduce visual friction and resemble an email that their founder, Dennis Rainey, might actually write. Then they took a new approach with the tone, making it very personal. The treatment copy talked about his wife, their history serving pastors, and the challenges they’ve seen firsthand. The ask was vey clear and direct to the reader, and provided just a single link to the donation page, with no button.
This led to a donation page that led with a headline that extends an offer to the reader: Invest in God’s work by strengthening a pastor’s marriage. This copy removed the story, with the hypothesis that the third-party story with no real connection and anonymized names might detract from the clarity of the value proposition. The treatment added a bulleted list of what the pastor couple would receive at a Weekend to Remember event, and made a clear ask to the reader. The visual separation from the green box was removed, and the donation form was shortened by bringing multiple fields (i.e. first name and last name) onto the same line, which gave the perception of less friction, while still collecting the same information.
They split their file evenly (making sure to evenly split donors and non-donors) and sent one version to each segment. The control email linked to the control page and the treatment email to the treatment page to preserve the integrity of the test throughout the entire appeal flow.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment donation flow produced a statistically valid 272.1% increase in donor conversion. This strong leap forward was due to a few identifiable conversion factors:
Reduction of visual friction in the control email led to more readers engaging with the copy.
Personal tone in the email increased motivation to give.
Decreased friction on the landing page increased likelihood to complete a donation
However, since all of these optimizations were made in one fell swoop, we cannot know exactly how each change contributed to the total increase. That said, this experiment provides a new benchmark for email appeal testing for FamilyLife—one that can be optimized in its own right.

experiments.title: How matching gift copy affects clickthrough rate on a modal popup
experiments.researchQuestion: How does matching gift language affect clickthrough rate on a modal popup window?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/26/2018 - 12/28/2017
FamilyLife was in the final week of their year-end campaign, and launched a modal popup to point web traffic to the donation page. The popup contained a countdown clock as well as urgency-focused message. The default version of the popup had language that echoed the headline, but they wondered if including matching gift language would increase clickthrough rate.
They created a treatment that kept urgent language, but added in the fact that the gift would be matched dollar-for-dollar. They launched a test to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment (with matching gift language) produced a 56% increase in clickthrough rate. This test revealed that while urgency is key to any message with a deadline (especially at the end of the year), incentive can provide additional lift for action oriented copy.
In the control, the potential donor is presented with four elements to inspire them to click through and give: an urgent headline, a countdown clock, additional copy above the button, and the button copy. But in the treatment, the matching gift language provides a fifth element, without leaving out any of the previous four—incentive.
Many times, calls-to-action have one of these elements, but not the other. This test shows the power of combining the two.
