experiments.title: How an impact pop-up Dear Reader message affects donor conversion on article pages
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that displaying the “Dear Reader” message as an impact pop-up rather than at the end of the article will increase donor conversion rates by capturing attention earlier and making the donation ask more immediate and visible.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/26/2025 - 03/26/2025
FamilyLife is a Christian organization focused on strengthening marriages and families by providing resources, events, and guidance rooted in biblical principles, helping people navigate the challenges of family life and fostering lasting, healthy relationships.​
In previous campaigns, FamilyLife has utilized “Dear Reader” messages at the end of article pages—a tactic that has proven successful in increasing donation rates. To build upon this success, we are testing an alternative approach: implementing an impact pop-up “Dear Reader” message that appears shortly after the page loads, eliminating the need for users to scroll to the end of the article. Our hypothesis is that the immediacy and visibility of the pop-up will capture users’ attention more effectively, thereby increasing the likelihood of securing donations.​
For this test, we are promoting the devotional “You’re Going to Make It” by Lysa TerKeurst, a resource that has demonstrated strong performance in FamilyLife’s Facebook advertising campaigns. The experiment is structured as a 50/50 split: half of the site traffic encounters the traditional end-of-article “Dear Reader” message (Control), while the other half experiences the impact pop-up “Dear Reader” message (Treatment).
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment ran for 30 days, from February 26 to March 26. While the results did not reach statistical validity, the impact pop-up "Dear Reader" message (Treatment) showed a positive trend, leading to a 49.5% increase in donor conversion rate with an 85.9% level of confidence compared to the end-of-article "Dear Reader" message (Control).

Although the overall conversion rate remained low, the pop-up format demonstrated a higher engagement potential, suggesting that making the donation ask more immediate and visible may help drive conversions.

Next Steps: Since the results were not statistically valid, retesting this approach in different contexts—such as other article pages, adjusting pop-up timing, or refining the message—could help validate the learning. Further optimizations to the design or placement of the pop-up may also enhance its effectiveness in future tests.

experiments.title: How defaulting to recurring gift with a partner’s benefits call-out on a donation page impacts recurring donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that emphasizing the exclusive benefits of becoming a FamilyLife Partner will increase the number of recurring donations because it provides added value to donors, strengthening their connection to the organization and making the recurring gift feel more rewarding and meaningful.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 11/08/2024
FamilyLife is a Christian organization dedicated to strengthening marriages and families through biblically-based resources, events, and guidance, supporting individuals as they build enduring, healthy relationships. Previously, we tested a recurring gift default on FamilyLife’s premium donation pages, which led to a 0.53% increase in recurring donations with a 99.9% level of confidence. Based on this success, the recurring default has been implemented as a permanent feature across the donation microsite. In this experiment, we aim to test a new approach to the recurring gift call-out, focusing on language that highlights the exclusive benefits of becoming a FamilyLife Partner, to assess if this approach will further encourage monthly giving.
If we emphasize the exclusive benefits of becoming a FamilyLife Partner (e.g., access to insider ministry updates, private events, and a curated library), then users will be more likely to commit to a recurring gift, as the added value and connection to the FamilyLife community will increase their perceived return on investment and loyalty.
The test involved two variations on the donation page. The Control version used a recurring gift call-out that stated, “Equip 140 families with practical insights to strengthen the relationships that matter most.” The Treatment version introduced a new call-out focused on membership benefits: “Receive exclusive benefits: insider ministry updates, invitation-only events, access to our curated library, and membership to our private Partners-only network.” This A/B test was split 50/50 and conducted on their best performing premium donation pages: Seeing Beautiful Again, 52 Weeks in the Word, and the Ferret Flush Couples Pack.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The experiment aimed to determine if emphasizing exclusive benefits for recurring donors would increase the number of monthly donations on FamilyLife’s premium donation pages. However, partway through the test, two of the three offers (Seeing Beautiful Again, 52 Weeks in the Word, and Ferret Flush Couples Pack) were turned off due to budget limitations. As a result, traffic to the test was reduced, which impacted our ability to achieve a conclusive result.
Recurring Donations Results: The Control outperformed the Treatment in terms of recurring donations. The Control had a 0.59% conversion rate (13 recurring gifts from 2,210 samples), while the Treatment had a 0.30% conversion rate (7 recurring gifts from 2,366 samples), representing a relative decrease of 49.7% at an 86.1% level of confidence. This difference did not reach statistical significance, so the results are inconclusive.
Overall Transactions Results: When examining all transactions, the Control had a 4.8% conversion rate (107 total gifts from 2,210 samples) compared to a 4.9% conversion rate in the Treatment (115 total gifts from 2,366 samples). This represents a slight relative increase of 0.4% in the Treatment, with a very low level of confidence (2.4%). This result also did not validate, indicating that the new messaging had no meaningful effect on overall transactions.
Theories on Donor Response:
Mismatch with Motivation: FamilyLife donors may be motivated more by the immediate impact of their giving rather than exclusive, long-term benefits. The Control message, which directly highlighted the impact of equipping families, may have resonated more with donors’ desire to see their contribution create tangible change. The Treatment, which shifted focus to membership perks, may have unintentionally distracted from this primary motivation.Perceived Complexity: The mention of exclusive benefits and a private network may have introduced an element of complexity or commitment that was unappealing. Donors may respond best to messages that feel straightforward and immediately impactful. The “Partners-only network” or “curated library” might not have been clearly valuable in this context and could have made the ask feel less accessible.Different Expectations for Monthly Giving: Some donors may view recurring giving as a simple way to support the organization consistently, without needing additional incentives. This audience might value the ease and simplicity of monthly giving over exclusive access, making the Partner benefits message less motivating.

experiments.title: How aligning a premium offer with seasonality during CYE impacts clicks and donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a seasonally-aligned premium offer in the Dear Listener for visitors on FamilyLife’s podcast pages will achieve an increase in clicks to the premium donation page because the seasonally-aligned premium offer will feel more relevant and appealing to the viewer.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/20/2023 - 12/01/2023
During their Calendar Year-End (CYE) fundraising season, FamilyLife has used “Dear Listener” elements on their podcast pages to convert their web visitors into donors. Before CYE began, we had running a Dear Listener that promoted a card game premium for married couples. But we wondered if aligning the premium offer with the time of the year would make it more relevant and acquire more donors.
The premium offer that FamilyLife was promoting during CYE was a 52-week devotional book. This book was included as an incentive in their CYE campaign emails and on the campaign’s donation page. We decided to run this offer as our Treatment to see if it would get more users to engage and make a gift to FamilyLife.
Our hypothesis was that the devotional premium would achieve an increase in clicks to the premium donation page because it would feel more relevant to the web visitors; this is because December is a popular season for people to get devotionals that they can begin in the new year.
We ran this as an A/B test in VWO for ~1.5 weeks. The Control Dear Listener promoted the game and pointed to the game’s donation page, and the Treatment Dear Listener promoted the devotional book and pointed to its donation page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running this test for a week and a half, we found that the seasonally-aligned devotional premium actually to a -31.4% decrease in clicks to the donation page, with an 87% level of confidence. This test didn't meet the 95% level of confidence that's required to consider it a fully valid result, but we turned the test off early due to the poor trending results and the importance of the Calendar Year-End fundraising season.
There are a few different angles that we can test with this idea in the future:
Would we see a different result if we tested a seasonally-aligned offer during a different time of year (ex: Valentine's Day, Easter, etc.)?Would we see a different result if the control and treatment are the same type of premium offer (ex: both are books, or both are games, etc.)?

experiments.title: How a premium offer dear reader CTA on podcast pages impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a dear reader premium offer on podcast pages will increase donor conversion
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/17/2023 - 08/14/2023
FamilyLife has a number of popular podcasts with thousands of visitors a month coming to their site to tune in. In reviewing these podcast pages, we hypothesized that we could provide further value for visitors and increase donor conversion by presenting a premium offer in a “dear listener” message on the page.
In our prior testing, we found that an end-of-article dear reader would increase donor conversion on article pages by reducing the friction of making a gift and increasing the appeal of the donation CTA through more space for value-driven copy. We believed that a dear listener message would achieve the same result by providing a way for listeners to engage in a deeper way with what they were hearing and receive a valuable resource in exchange for their gift.
We selected their most popular premium offer, Seeing Beautiful Again and created a dear-listener message on the podcast pages to promote it. We split traffic evenly and ran the experiment for a full month.
experiments.keyLearnings: The dear listener message improved donor conversion significantly. While we weren't able to validate this increase to a 95% or greater level of confidence, the lack of a negative impact from the treatment makes us confident that featuring a dear-listener premium offer is more likely than not to improve donor conversion. For our next experiment we will test this premium offer against a different one to see which sort of offer best aligns with the visitors to these podcasts. We will also continue to test the placement of the message on the page to see how that impacts donor conversion and user friction.

experiments.title: How a sticky donate button on article pages with a slide-out donor appeal impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a sticky donate button with a slide-out CTA featuring additional value proposition copy will increase donor conversion by strengthening the value proposition of the sticky donate button.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/23/2023 - 03/31/2023
FamilyLife is a Christian ministry serving families worldwide. They had previously tested a sticky donate button on article pages that floated on the right-hand side of the page with a pulsing heart and linked to the main donation page. This sticky button improved donor conversion. They wanted to test a new version of that button with an expanded value proposition that would appear in a slide-out when the button was clicked. This they posited would enable visitors to receive a reason to donate before visiting the donation page itself. Visitors could then choose to give or dismiss the message altogether.
For the experiment, they wanted to test two different value propositions. One would focus on FamilyLife’s standard appeal message about making a gift to support families in crisis. The other would focus on the perceived value the visitor received from FamilyLife this year and ask them to make a gift to keep FamilyLife here for them and their family.
Originally these two variations were going to be tested against the prior winning sticky donate button that lacked a slide-out CTA message. Due to a setup error, the control experience lacked a sticky donate button altogether, so it became an experiment between these two competing value proposition slide-outs and no sticky donate button.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We found that inviting visitors to reflect on the value FamilyLife provided them and then asking them to donate to keep FamilyLife there for them and their family was far more motivational than asking them to make a gift to help other families. We hypothesize this is because it is harder for a potential donor to think about the needs of unidentified "families in critical need" than it is to remember their needs and how FamilyLife has been their for them.
We will continue testing this value proposition to further refine it and identify key motivational elements for donor conversion. Our next test will be this slide-out CTA sticky button against the former control experience of just a sticky donate button without a slide-out message.

experiments.title: How a “complete your gift” popup during a Fiscal Year End Campaign affects donor revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a “complete your gift” popup targeting visitors who choose a gift amount but abandon the donation page will increase donor conversion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/24/2022 - 09/04/2022
FamilyLife is a Christian ministry serving families worldwide. For their august Fiscal Year-End campaign, they were seeing a decrease in donor revenue and we wanted to see if we could turn that around in the final days of the campaign. We decided to test a popup what would show if a visitor visited any donation page on their site and chose a gift amount but then abandoned the page before competing their donation. This popup would then fire when they went anywhere else on the site and offer them the prompt to complete their X gift or indicate they weren’t ready to do so. If they chose to, it would reload the donation page so they could complete the transaction.
We split the abandoned donation page traffic 50/50 and ran the experiment for about 11 days.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We were only able to record an extremely marginal impact on donor conversion for the popup experience, however the impact on revenue was found in all primary channels to the website. While we were just under a 95% LOC for all traffic, we found a similar increase on all other channels including a 41% increase for direct traffic at a 97% LOC.
The popup reduced friction for these donors, causing them to give a higher amount when they revisited the donation page. Since this ran during the Fiscal Year End campaign, we want to relaunch it in September to measure impact without a high urgency campaign skewing results one way or another.
Other revenue impact measured included:
Social visitors had a 77.0% increase in revenue with a 91% level of confidence.Desktop visitors had a 20.7% increase in revenue with an 88% level of confidence.Mobile visitors had a 13.8% increase in revenue with an 88% level of confidence.New  visitors had a 16.6% increase in revenue with a 91% level of confidence.Returning  visitors had a 17.9% increase in revenue with an 86% level of confidence.

experiments.title: The Impact of a Higher Suggested Gift Array on a Premium Offer Donation Page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by slightly increasing the suggest gift amounts on the donation page’s giving array, we will increase revenue for FamilyLife’s premium offer.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/11/2022 - 04/25/2022
FamilyLife uses premium offers as their primary acquisition source for new donors on Facebook. Their most popular offer is the Seeing Beautiful Again book that is given with a donation to the ministry. The vast majority of traffic to this donation page is from new visitors who saw an ad and clicked through to the page. We hypothesized that a slightly higher suggested gift amount on the giving array would increase donor revenue. We increased each option by $5.
NOTE: In the results below, the treatment was mislabeled as “lower” array when it was in fact higher.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We were not able to reach a greater than 95% level of confidence for any of the visitor segments during the test, however we were able to achieve the highest confidence on our new visitors audience, the largest audience for the premium. New Visitors gave 22% more revenue on average with the higher array. Returning visitors however, gave 33% les (with an 82% LOC). While this negative result for returning visitors is less reliable with its lower LOC, our hypothesis is that new visitors don't have a specific gift amount in mind when they visit. The new array serves as the MSRP for the premium and more choose the higher amount because they have no other frame of reference for how much the premium may be worth. Returning visitors, however, are likely used to "paying" less for the premium and are less likely to convert due to the "sticker shock" of the higher array.
More testing is needed to confirm whether this hypothesis is correct.  We also noticed after running the test that we preselected the $30 amount on the control but preselected the lower option ($20) on the treatment. This also could have colored the results and muddied the test. Before this treatment is used for new visitors to this or other offers, it should be tested further without a mismatch in which option is preselected.

experiments.title: How communal language on a high-urgency campaign sticky bar affects revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: Communal language will increase revenue during a high urgency campaigns.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/07/2021 - 05/30/2021
FamilyLife was running a matching gift campaign with a focus on recurring giving. Since communal language—copy that shows the reader that they are united with the organization in the most important beliefs and causes that they care for, all spoken with a familiarity of friends—works well for other faith-based organizations. We’d seen lifts of donations and revenue during high-urgency campaign and wanted to test if this would benefit FamilyLife as well. We tested a 50/50 split in traffic on their website.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Overall, there was a directional lift in revenue. This did not fully validate because of the 23% decrease in revenue for new visitors that pulled down the full validation of a 59% increase in revenue for returning visitors.
Our hypothesis is that communal language, invoking the tone of friendship and care, works very well with returning visitors because they are more closely acquainted with the organization. Whereas a intimate tone with a new acquaintance will not resonate.
Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
Organic Search visitors had a 21.5% increase in revenue with an 86% level of confidence.Paid Search visitors had a 57.7% decrease in revenue with a 98% level of confidence.Referral visitors had an 89.5% increase in revenue with an 88% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How removing elements of friction and strengthening the value proposition impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Which donation page would increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/07/2017 - 09/26/2017
The primary donation page for FamilyLife used a large image at the top of it with their traditional value proposition copy on it. They recently developed their value proposition and wanted to test a radical redesign of their primary donation page that tested this copy and removed other elements of the page.
experiments.keyLearnings: The radical redesign increased donor conversion by 14.3%. By removing elements of friction on the page and strengthening their value proposition, we were able to not only increase the number of people giving a gift, but we were able to also motivate them to give more generously resulting in a 94.4% increase (77% valid) in revenue as well because the average gift increased.

experiments.title: How adding scripture reinforces value proposition in an email appeal
experiments.researchQuestion: Does adding scripture reinforce the value proposition to the donor?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/15/2017 - 08/16/2017
FamilyLife was running a fiscal year end high urgency campaign and wanted to test the impact of adding a scripture reference into the email on donations.

experiments.title: How adding a testimonial to an email affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Does the addition of a third-party testimonial increase donor conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/26/2017 - 08/27/2017
FamilyLife was running a high urgency moneybomb campaign. They wanted to test whether adding a testimonial to an email would increase people’s motivation to give and their belief in the value proposition of the organization.
experiments.keyLearnings: The testimonial increased conversion by 167%. This email only reached 91% validation, but it was clear to readers that the quote from an outside source was genuine and motivated more people to give by supporting the value proposition.

experiments.title: How removing a banner in an email appeal impact donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Would removing the banner from the email copy increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/13/2017 - 12/20/2017
For a FamilyLife email appeal during their year-end giving campaign, they inserted a banner in the body of the copy that served as a call-to-action. This banner then would take people to the donation page. We hypothesized that we might be able to increase donor conversion if we removed the banner and used text. We split the email file and tested this.
experiments.keyLearnings:
By removing the banner from the email, we decreased clicks by 46%. While this metric is important, we ultimately care about donor conversion. Even though there was a 46% decrease in clicks from the email that didn’t have a banner we were able to increase donor conversion by 90%! The donor conversion metric didn’t quite validate (89% valid), but it is leaning towards validity and is at a point that we feel confident enough to draw learnings from.
This tells us that even though we got more people to click through from the control email with the banner, those people were not as highly motivated donors as the people who clicked the text and raw link. The visual piece in the email created an easy click-through point for people, but those people weren’t ready to donate once they got to the donation page. The banner was also a visual piece of friction that possibly prevented people from reading the whole value proposition. By removing the banner, more people read the copy and we were able to fully communicate the value proposition and motivate people to give once they got to the call-to-action at the bottom of the email.
This test also shows why it is important to not base our testing decisions off of up-stream metrics when we can. If we would have done that, we would have championed the version that increased clicks but decreased donations.

experiments.title: How adding value proposition to a homepage banner affects donation conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Does a value prop-focused banner increase donations more than a match-focused banner?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/06/2017 - 12/13/2017
FamilyLife had a high-urgency banner focused on their 2 million dollar matching gift for their year-end campaign. They wanted to test whether a banner that focused more on the value a gift provided would increase donations more than a match-focused banner.
experiments.keyLearnings: The test did not validate, but the treatment banner produced a 21% decrease in donations. Because there were multiple factors tested in the banner, it’s hard to say exactly which one produced a decrease, but the removal of matching gift language and a less urgent color on the banner likely contributed to the decrease, because the matching gift language proved to be a motivating factor for FL donors in other testing.

experiments.title: How match language on a popup affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Does match language on a popup increase donor conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/14/2017 - 12/19/2017
FamilyLife was running their year-end campaign and wanted to test match language on their website pop-up versus no match language.
experiments.keyLearnings: The experiment did not validate, but saw a trend towards a 19% increase in gifts with match language on the popup. People were more likely to give when they know that their gift will be matched and make a greater impact.

experiments.title: How splitting the sticky slide-out donation ask into two steps impacts donor conversion on FamilyLife’s website
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that splitting the donation ask into two steps will increase conversion rates. By first asking whether FamilyLife has provided value and then following up with a donation request, we reduce cognitive load and create a smoother, less overwhelming user experience, which will lead to higher engagement and more conversions.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/26/2025
FamilyLife, a nonprofit dedicated to strengthening families through biblically-based resources, uses a persistent sticky donation slide-out on article pages to encourage giving. The Control (current version) features a single message asking users if FamilyLife had provided them with $25 worth of valuable resources and, if so, invites them to donate. Users can select “Yes” to proceed to the donation page or “No” to close the slide-out.
We previously tested this dual-ask approach during FamilyLife’s year-end campaign, where the Treatment led to a 50.9% increase in conversions but did not reach statistical significance, achieving only a 68.9% level of confidence. Despite this, the results were promising enough that FamilyLife chose to implement it for the remainder of the campaign due to the urgency of year-end giving. Given the directional improvement, we wanted to retest this approach in a non-urgent campaign setting to further evaluate its impact.
In this experiment, we tested a modified version (Treatment) that split the question into two steps. The first step only asked if FamilyLife had provided $25 worth of value. If users clicked “Yes,” they were then shown the donation request. If they clicked “No,” the slide-out closed immediately.
We hypothesized that this two-step approach would improve conversion rates by reducing cognitive load, making the initial interaction feel less transactional, and increasing engagement before presenting the donation ask. The experiment was structured as an A/B test with a 50/50 traffic split between the Control (current sticky donate button) and the Treatment (split-question version), measuring whether this adjustment led to higher donor conversions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment ran for 30 days. The Treatment (dual ask) led to a 65% increase in donations compared to the Control (single ask). However, the result reached a 77% level of confidence, meaning it did not fully validate at the typical 95% threshold but showed promising potential.

The Control (standard sticky slide-out) generated 9 transactions, while the Treatment (dual ask) resulted in 15 transactions.

Similar to the previous test conducted during FamilyLife’s high-urgency calendar year-end campaign, we believe the dual ask improved conversions by reducing cognitive overload. By first asking a low-effort question about the value of FamilyLife’s resources, users were more inclined to engage. Those who clicked “Yes” then encountered the donation request in a way that felt more natural and less transactional. The additional step may have also given users more time to process the ask, leading to a higher likelihood of conversion.

Given the positive directional impact across multiple tests, we recommend retesting the dual ask in future campaigns to further validate its effect on conversions. Additionally, there are opportunities to explore other optimizations for the sticky donate button, such as presenting a content offer or premium or updating the copy to enhance the value proposition.

experiments.title: How testing a dual action slide-out on article pages impacts donor conversion during FamilyLife’s high urgency calendar year-end campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that splitting the donation ask into two steps will increase conversion rates. By first asking whether FamilyLife has provided value and then following up with a donation request, we reduce cognitive load and create a smoother, less overwhelming user experience, which will lead to higher engagement and more conversions.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/08/2025
FamilyLife, a nonprofit dedicated to strengthening families through biblically-based resources, conducted this experiment as part of their high urgency calendar year-end campaign. FamilyLife uses a sticky donation slide-out that is always visible on article pages. This slide-out prompts users with the message: “Has FamilyLife given you and your family $25 worth of practical resources and biblical insights this year? If so, can you take a moment to donate to ensure that we’re always here for you when your family needs encouragement and support?” Users can then click “Yes” to be directed to the donation page, or “No” to close the element.
For this campaign, we wanted to test whether splitting the question into two parts would lead to higher conversion rates. In the Treatment, when the user first clicks on the sticky slide-out, they would only see the first part of the question—“Has FamilyLife given you $25 worth of practical resources and biblical insights this year?” If they click “Yes,” the second part of the message would appear, asking for a donation. If they click “No,” the element would close without prompting for a donation.
We believe that splitting the ask into two steps could improve engagement and conversion for several reasons. First, by breaking down the question, we reduce the initial cognitive load on users, making the first interaction feel less like a commitment and more like a simple, low-effort engagement. This might encourage more people to click “Yes.” Once they’ve answered affirmatively, they’ll then be presented with a clearer, more straightforward ask for a donation, which could feel less transactional and more natural. Additionally, by limiting the ask to one step at a time, we avoid overwhelming users with too much information or too big of a request at once, which could increase the likelihood of a conversion.
The Control group used the standard, single-message approach, while the Treatment group implemented the dual ask. This experiment was set up as a split A/B test with the traffic split 50/50 between the Control (standard slide-out) and the Treatment (dual ask). The goal was to determine if breaking the ask into two steps would create a smoother, more engaging donation flow, leading to higher conversions. Both the Control and Treatment groups used the exact same language in their messaging and was tested on the article pages.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment ran for 21 days. The Treatment (dual ask) led to a 50.9% increase in conversions, but with a 68.90% level of confidence, which means it didn’t fully validate. Despite not reaching the typical 95% confidence threshold, we decided to roll out the Treatment early due to the high urgency of the calendar year-end campaign.
The Control (standard sticky slide-out) generated 10 transactions, while the Treatment (dual ask) resulted in 15 transactions.
We believe the dual ask led to more conversions because it reduced cognitive overload by presenting a lower-effort, initial question. This likely made users more inclined to engage with the content, as the first interaction felt less like a commitment. Additionally, by breaking the ask into two steps, users had more time to process the request, leading to a higher likelihood of converting once they expressed interest in donating. The treatment also benefited from the fact that the second ask for a donation only appeared after the user clicked "Yes"—a step that could have made the donation request feel more natural and less transactional.
We recommend retesting the dual ask in FamilyLife’s next high-urgency campaigns to gather more data and validate its impact on conversions. Additionally, there may be an opportunity to explore this format as a permanent feature, especially if future testing confirms its effectiveness over a longer period and across various campaigns.

experiments.title: How the placement of a Dear Listener message vs. an Impact Pop-up on a donation page impacts donor conversion during a year end campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using the Impact Pop-up for FamilyLife’s calendar year-end campaign will increase donor conversion better than the Dear Listener message because the pop-up creates a more immediate sense of urgency and engagement, encouraging users to take action right away.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/02/2023 - 12/16/2023
FamilyLife, a nonprofit dedicated to strengthening families through biblically-based resources, ran this experiment as part of their high urgency calendar year-end campaign, which began on December 2nd. Historically, FamilyLife has relied on a “Dear Listener” tactic at the end of their content—an approach that appeals directly to their audience and has proven successful in driving donations during previous high urgency campaigns. For this test, however, we sought to explore whether a different tactic could outperform this traditional method in terms of donor conversion.
To determine whether a more interactive and urgent format would yield higher conversions, we tested the Impact Pop-up against the “Dear Listener” message. The “Dear Listener” message was used in the Control group, maintaining the traditional approach where the appeal is made at the end of the content. In the Treatment group, we implemented the Impact Pop-up, which was triggered during the user’s session and emphasized the urgency of supporting the mission. The pop-up also featured an image of a family reading a Bible to reinforce the message and visually engage users.
This experiment was set up as a split A/B test in VWO, with the traffic being split 50/50 between the Control (Dear Listener) and the Treatment (Impact Pop-up). We tested these tactics on their podcast pages. Both versions used similar messaging, but the key differences were the format and the visibility of the appeal.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The experiment ran from December 2nd to December 16th, a two-week period, and although it did not reach the ideal 30-day threshold for full validation, the Impact Pop-up showed a promising 51.8% increase in conversions with a 67.6% likelihood of conversion (LoC) compared to the Dear Listener message. The Dear Listener generated 9 transactions, while the Impact Pop-up generated 14 transactions. While the result is compelling, it was still a bit too early to validate fully, but given the urgency of the campaign, the decision was made to roll out the Impact Pop-up.

The Impact Pop-up likely performed better because it created a sense of urgency by appearing during the user’s session, prompting immediate action. Unlike the Dear Listener message, which required users to scroll down the page to see it, the Impact Pop-up was triggered after 7 seconds of page engagement, ensuring that most visitors to the page would see it. This guaranteed visibility likely contributed to higher engagement and conversion, as users didn’t need to actively seek out the message. The emotional appeal of the image of a family reading the Bible further reinforced the urgency, making the call-to-action feel more relevant and personal for users.

Given the success of the Impact Pop-up in this initial period, we recommend rolling it out as the primary tactic for future high urgency campaigns. However, since the experiment didn’t run long enough to validate fully, we suggest retesting, continuing to monitor its performance closely, and conducting follow-up testing to further optimize the pop-up’s design and messaging. Additionally, testing with a larger sample size and longer duration could help validate its effectiveness across different user segments.

experiments.title: How adding a testimonial box with a social proof headline impacts donor conversions during a year-end campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a testimonial box with the headline “Make an Impact on readers like __” to the right side of FamilyLife’s donation page will increase donor conversions by providing social proof and illustrating the tangible impact of donations, thereby fostering trust and emotional connection with potential donors.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/20/2024 - 12/31/2024
FamilyLife is a nonprofit organization dedicated to providing biblically-based resources and support to families. This experiment was conducted during their high-urgency calendar year-end campaign, a critical time for driving donor engagement and conversions.
Historically, FamilyLife’s donation pages have focused on clearly presenting the campaign’s messaging and donation options without additional elements like testimonials. For this test, we wanted to explore whether incorporating social proof could enhance donor trust and urgency.
To test this, we added a testimonial box featuring a real quote from a FamilyLife supporter, paired with a social proof headline: “Make an Impact on Readers like Giovanni and Alana!” This headline aimed to create a sense of community and inspire donors to contribute by showcasing the tangible impact of their support.
The Control was the standard donation page without a testimonial, and the Treatment included the testimonial box prominently placed near the donation form. This was a split A/B test built in VWO with 50/50 split traffic, ensuring an equal number of users experienced both the Control and the Treatment.
The goal of this test was to determine whether the addition of social proof could increase donor conversion rates during this critical fundraising period.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The Treatment featuring the testimonial box with the headline, "Make an Impact on Readers like Giovanni and Alana!", led to a 6.2% decrease in transactions compared to the Control at a 70% level of confidence (LoC). While the results did not validate due to insufficient data over the testing period, the downward trend suggests that the testimonial box may have negatively impacted conversions.

We hypothesize that the addition of the testimonial box could have distracted users from focusing on the primary goal: completing the donation. While the testimonial and headline aimed to build trust and provide social proof, it may have introduced unnecessary cognitive load or drawn attention away from the donation form itself. Alternatively, the headline or the quote could have lacked relevance or resonance for some users, reducing its effectiveness as a motivator.

Next Steps:

Given the negative trend, we recommend retesting this concept with adjustments:
Rework the Design: Explore a more streamlined and less visually dominant placement for the testimonial box.
 	Refine the Content: Test different quotes or headlines that may better resonate with the audience or align more closely with the campaign’s urgency.
 	Alternative Social Proof Strategies: Consider integrating testimonials in a subtler way, such as below the donation form or as part of an impact statement, to avoid distracting users.

experiments.title: How a premium offer chat CTA on podcast pages impacts donor conversion during a high urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that utilizing a premium offer chat widget with a conversational tone and engaging design will increase donor conversion rates during the high urgency campaign by enhancing the visibility of the call-to-action and creating a more personal appeal.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/01/2024 - 08/22/2024
FamilyLife is a nonprofit organization committed to strengthening families through various resources, including their engaging podcast series. As part of their Fiscal Year End (FYE) campaign, we are testing two different messaging approaches on the podcast pages to encourage donations.
Historically, the “Dear Listener” message has proven successful in driving donations. However, we want to explore whether a more dynamic approach, utilizing a chat widget, might enhance engagement and boost donor conversion rates. This experiment follows a successful test conducted in July with the “Seeing Beautiful Again” campaign, which achieved a 187.9% increase in donor conversion. We aim to see if a similar impact can be replicated with a different premium during the FYE campaign.
We believe that the conversational and eye-catching design of the chat message widget will increase donor conversion during the FYE campaign by improving the visibility of the call-to-action (CTA) and humanizing the appeal.
The control version of the podcast page featured the traditional “Dear Listener” premium message, which included an image of the premium offer, Unoffendable. The treatment version showcased the premium message chat widget, featuring an image of a FamilyLife member. Most of the language remained consistent between the two versions, and traffic was split 50/50 to measure differences in donation conversion rates accurately.
We also tested this on their Article Pages.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The experiment results indicated that the treatment group featuring the premium offer chat widget achieved a conversion rate of 0.39%, compared to the control group’s rate of 0.32%. This represents a relative difference of 24%. However, with a level of confidence of only 43.6%, these results were not statistically significant.

Despite the positive trend in donor conversion rates observed with the chat widget, the lack of robust statistical significance suggests that more data is needed to draw reliable conclusions. The test ran for 21 days, which may have limited the ability to fully assess the widget's effectiveness.

Next Steps:
Extended Testing Duration: Run the experiment for a longer period to gather more data, especially during different times of the month or year to account for seasonal variations in donor behavior.
 	A/B Test Variations of the Chat Widget: Experiment with different designs and messaging variations for the chat widget to identify elements that resonate better with the audience. Consider testing language, tone, and imagery to enhance engagement.
 	Analyze User Interaction: Utilize analytics tools to monitor how users interact with the chat widget (e.g., clicks, time spent, engagement) to identify any drop-off points or issues affecting conversion rates.

experiments.title: How switching the order in which information is presented affects conversion rate

rows.name: Switch order of the Main Donation Page

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by switching the order in which information is presented for our donors will achieve a higher conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/17/2021 - 10/24/2021
We tested this on FamilyLife’s main donation page. We noticed that when filling out your information, the last step to complete the donation process is adding your card information. We suspected that this could potentially cause friction due to all the internal decisions we are already making before hitting “make my gift”. Could be not having your card ready to fill out that information or perhaps your willingness to make that “commitment” decreases as you fill in other information.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
Referral visitors had a 57.3% decrease in donations with an 86% level of confidence.
 	Paid Search visitors had a 33.3% increase in donations with a 92% level of confidence.
 	Tablet visitors had a 100.0% decrease in donations with a 98% level of confidence.
 	35-44 visitors had a 100.0% decrease in donations with a 98% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How a sticky bar with a daily donor goal countdown impacts donor conversion on a high urgency campaign donation page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a sticky bar with a daily donor goal countdown on the donation page will increase donor conversion during a high urgency campaign
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/01/2023 - 05/20/2023
During FamilyLife’s Spring Recurring Campaign, they rely on a daily donor countdown in sticky bars and website calls to action to increase urgency and drive traffic to their donation page. We hypothesized that reinforcing this countdown on the page itself would add urgency and increase donor conversion.
The message would indicate how many new monthly donors were still needed before midnight that night.  When the number was reached, it would change to say, “We still need new monthly partners before 11:59pm to be here for you and other families in need.”
We split traffic evenly between both versions of the page and ran it for almost three weeks.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a sticky bar with a daily donor goal countdown on the donation page did not increase donor conversion during the high urgency campaign. The treatment group actually saw a decrease in FamilyLife - Recurring Donations compared to the control group.
While we could not validate this decrease, it was a sharp enough decline in conversion that we ended the experiment early so we wouldn't harm donor conversion during the critical campaign. It is possible that the message was more of a distraction than provided additional urgency to the page.

experiments.title: How converting donation form into a multi-step form impacts mobile donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that splitting the donation form into smaller steps will increase donor conversion for mobile traffic
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/06/2022 - 11/06/2022
FamilyLife is a Christian ministry serving families worldwide. They were wanting to test ways of increasing donor conversion, particularly for visitors using a mobile device. They hypothesized that the current form was too long for Mobile and caused visual friction and that a shorter form would lead to higher donor conversion for this traffic segment to the main donation page.
They ran the test for one month and found the following results.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After one month, we were unable to validate the impact of a 3-step form. There just wasn't enough of an impact and enough gifts during the test to validate. We will not roll out a 3-step form but will instead test other ways of removing friction and increasing the value proposition of the page. For example, adding additional value propositions around the form submission button or testing the impact of asking for credit card fees on the form.
We should also test in the future a step one of X feature so they know what step they are on.

experiments.title: How shorter copy in a site-wide sticky bar affects donations

rows.name: Sticky Bar with Daily Donor Countdown
Sticky Bar with Daily Donor Countdown Shorter Version

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that shorter copy for website visitors will achieve more donations.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/13/2021 - 09/04/2021
The team wondered if the long value proposition copy in a site-wide “Give Today” sticky bar was too long for people to quickly understand, and could be standing in the way of people clicking though to donate. So tested shorter value proposition copy to see if brevity would lead to more clarity and, ultimately, more donations.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The shorter value proposition copy did not significantly impact donations, and for many segments appeared to lean towards a decrease in performance.
From a Bayesian perspective... Version B has an 80% chance of out performing the control. The likelihood of the short-copy outperforming the control is low, and could actually lead to a decrease in performance.
Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
Tablet visitors had a 72.9% decrease in donations with a 97% level of confidence.25-34 visitors had a 46.3% decrease in donations with a 96% level of confidence.35-44 visitors had a 49.4% decrease in donations with a 92% level of confidence.65+ visitors had a 41.5% decrease in donations with an 85% level of confidence.

experiments.title: The impact of Fiscal Year End Language on the donation page for the fiscal year-end campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding fiscal year end language will increase donor conversion on the main donation page during the Fiscal Year-End campaign.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/11/2022 - 08/27/2022
FamilyLife is a Christian ministry serving families worldwide. For their august Fiscal Year-End campaign, they were using a premium offer, Find Your People and a donor goal. They wanted to see if adding value proposition on the donation page surrounding Fiscal Year-End and the importance of ending the year “strong” would increase donor conversion on the donation page. No other elements included this language, just the main donation page. The control page would discuss the donor goal and the premium, the treatment would include language about the fiscal year ending and why that matters to the work FamilyLife does to strengthen families worldwide.
We split traffic 50/50 and ran it for just over two weeks.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After 257 donations, there wasn't a clear improvement on donor conversion or revenue from the treatment. We did see an 80% Level Of Confidence for an improvement on revenue, but when digging into the segments, there were conflicting results between leading sources of traffic to their site and large gifts may have skewed these results. Due to the importance of ending the campaign strong and the desire to launch another test in the last days of the campaign (a sitewide popup that would prompt visitors to complete their donation) we ended this test.
We will see if we can test this approach again next year, but add the Fiscal Year End language to the emails as well as to the donation page to see if this holistic approach increases conversion by keeping that language consistent from email to page.

experiments.title: How reinforcing the value of a premium offer impact donor conversion

rows.name: Reinforcing Value Preposition of the Book

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that reinforcing the value of a premium offer on the donation page will increase donor conversion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/16/2022 - 06/13/2022
FamilyLife is a Christian ministry serving families worldwide. FamilyLife uses multiple premium offers on social media to acquire new donors. Their most popular premium is the Seeing Beautiful Again Devotional. They wanted to see if reinforcing the value proposition of the premium would increase donor conversion by making it more appealing to donors. They did this by creating a treatment that included social proof of what other donors said about the resource as well as changing the headline of the page to be more value driven. Instead of, “Seeing Beautiful Again” the treatment headline read, ” Gain healthier ways to process your pain through truth-based perspectives,” which we believed better emphasized the value the resource would bring to donors. We split traffic 50/50 between both pages and ran the test for 25 days.
experiments.keyLearnings: The changes made to reinforce the value of the premium did not have a statistically significant impact on donor conversion or donor revenue. While we saw user segments having conflicting impact, higher donor conversion on new visitors vs returning visitors, no segment's impact was able to be validated. Instead of focusing on the value proposition of the premium, we will shift our strategy to testing tactical elements on the page to see if that will improve conversion.

experiments.title: The impact of reinforcing the value of a premium offer on donor conversion

rows.name: Reinforcing Value Preposition of the Book

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that reinforcing the value of a premium offer on the donation page will increase donor conversion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/16/2022 - 06/13/2022
FamilyLife is a Christian ministry serving families worldwide. FamilyLife uses multiple premium offers on social media to acquire new donors. Their most popular premium is the Seeing Beautiful Again Devotional. They wanted to see if reinforcing the value proposition of the premium would increase donor conversion by making it more appealing to donors. They did this by creating a treatment that included social proof of what other donors said about the resource as well as changing the headline of the page to be more value driven. Instead of, “Seeing Beautiful Again” the treatment headline read, ” Gain healthier ways to process your pain through truth-based perspectives,” which we believed better emphasized the value the resource would bring to donors. We split traffic 50/50 between both pages and ran the test for 25 days.
experiments.keyLearnings: The changes made to reinforce the value of the premium did not have a statistically significant impact on donor conversion or donor revenue. While we saw user segments having conflicting impact, higher donor conversion on new visitors vs returning visitors, no segment's impact was able to be validated. Instead of focusing on the value proposition of the premium, we will shift our strategy to testing tactical elements on the page to see if that will improve conversion.

experiments.title: How Asking Donors for a Donation Amount Based on Their Prior Giving Impacted Donor Conversion During a High Urgency Campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that asking donors for a donation amount based on their prior giving will increase donor conversion.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 05/10/2022
Live Action is a pro-life organization that uses online content and breaking-news stories to change hearts and minds on abortion and reveal abortion’s impact on everyone involved. During their 5-day Spring Monthly Donor High Urgency Campaign, Live Action wanted to see if they could increase donor conversion by asking each donor for a specific donation amount that was based on their prior giving history. Donors receiving this treatment would be thanked for their gift and told that instead of that amount, they could have an even greater impact by giving this new amount on an ongoing basis as monthly donors.
The Control version would say we needed 250 new monthly donors by midnight on April 30th. The treatment version would use a chart of standards AND the donor’s prior gift to calculate a giving amount and donor goal. Example:
Friend of Life, this is why I’m looking for 100 donors to step forward and give $10 by Saturday, April 30th. Can you be one of these donors who saves lives every single day? I know you have given as much as $10 in the past, but today I’m not asking for that much this one time. Instead, would you consider giving just 10 to start your monthly partnership? 2,300 babies face death every day.
We hypothesized that this approach that combined a chart of standards AND donors prior to giving history would increase conversion. We ensured that the customized giving amount would push to the donation page and prepopulate the donor form.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Unfortunately, due to the small sample size, we were not able to approach statistical reliability. However, we did validate this approach with the lapsed donor file during the same campaign.

People primarily give to people, not organizations or causes. By reminding the donor of their last gift and talking about how this new amount could extend the impact of their generosity further, we increased donor conversion. We will update this analysis with a breakdown of the reactivation rates by donor segment (broadbase and midlevel) to see if this approach had a greater impact on one audience over another.

We will also take every opportunity we can to give donors tangible appeals that are directly linked to their history of support and relationship with the organization.

experiments.title: How ordering a donation form into steps impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Can we increase donor conversion by breaking the donation form into ordered steps?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/16/2020 - 10/17/2020
Through previous testing with other organizations, we found that ordering the donation form into steps has led to an increase in conversion rate on donation pages. Knowing this, we wanted to see the impact that this tactic would have on FamilyLife’s primary donation page. To test into this, we developed a treatment that took each section of the donation form and made them into steps – “walking” people through the donation pathway.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Overall, there was no difference in donor conversion. As we dug into the data a bit more, we found some interesting results within individual segments. Take a look:
New visitors had a 42.4% decrease in donations with a 98% level of confidence.Returning visitors had a 49.0% increase in donations with a 97% level of confidence.
These two segments, new and returning, had opposite results. This is why the overall results were flat. Our hypothesis in the decrease from new visitors is that the steps actually created friction to the giving process causing the page to look like MORE work to fill out (since there were steps to it), rather than our hypothesis of thinking that the steps would help the giving process. Returning visitors were more familiar with the donation page and giving process, so the steps may have helped them get through the form faster - resulting in the increase in donor conversion.
This shows us that the motivation of users is dramatically different and implementing new tactics like this can potentially have a significant impact - positive and negative - on certain segments.

experiments.title: How user focused messaging impacts donor conversion from the donate button
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a user focused message increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/15/2021 - 08/30/2021
After FamilyLife launched their new brand, they added some of their new brand messaging to the primary navigation header next to the donate button. The messaging read, “Because Family Matters Most”. We hypothesized that a message that was more user focused might help increase conversion from the donation button.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running this experiment for six weeks, we weren't able to validate the overall results. That said, on desktop devices there was a 13.7% decrease in donor conversion with a 89% level of confidence, but on mobile devices there was a 22% increase in donor conversion with a 94% level of confidence. This difference in conversion results is why the experiment didn't validate overall.
These findings are interesting in that mobile traffic is much more motivated by user centric messaging. We hypothesize that the placement and presence of this copy is also so much more visible on mobile devices which resulted in mobile visitors who were maybe on the fence about donating, made the decision to donate after seeing the messaging that had a clearer, user focused call-to-action.

experiments.title: How making gift type the first decision point affects donor acquisition on premium offers
experiments.researchQuestion: Clarifying gift type at the beginning of the form will not harm donor acquisition.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/12/2021 - 05/09/2021
FamilyLife found great success using tabbed donation forms on their main donation page. But before rolling this experience out to all of their donation pages, we wanted to ensure that this user experience did not not harm donor acquisition on acquisition offers. So we tested this on one of their most high performing premium offers, A Quiet Place. This was a 50/50 AB split test using an Optimize redirect. So half of the traffic saw the original format and the other half of traffic saw the tabbed form.
experiments.keyLearnings: As we were hoping, the tabbed form did not harm new donor conversion in any significant way. Neither revenue nor donations saw a valid decrease in this test. The next step is to roll this page out to their microsite.

experiments.title: How communicating the impact of a person’s gift through a sticky bar can impact donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Can we increase recurring donor conversion from the homepage by communicating the impact of a person’s gift on a sticky bar?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/23/2018 - 05/31/2018
FamilyLife ran a high-urgency campaign during the month of May in an effort to increase recurring donor conversion. We hypothesized that we might be able to turn some of the website traffic into recurring donors by presenting a sticky bar on the bottom of the homepage that communicated how many families a person would be able to reach with a gift.
experiments.keyLearnings: We weren't able to validate this experiment during the campaign. This experiment had an interesting concept and should be tested again. We propose testing this approach for one-time gifts during neutral giving seasons.

experiments.title: How pre-selecting the monthly recurring gift option impacts recurring donor conversion.

rows.name: Added Call to Action + Recurring Gift Default (1)

experiments.researchQuestion: Would pre-selecting the monthly gift option increase recurring donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/17/2018 - 06/02/2018
FamilyLife launched a recurring gift campaign. On the donation page they pushed people to, donors were given two options for their gift – a one-time gift, or a monthly gift. The primary goal of this campaign was to get more recurring donors. We hypothesized that we might be able to increase recurring donors if we get rid of the one-time gift check box and pre-select the monthly gift option.
experiments.keyLearnings: We weren’t able to validate the experiment, but we did get a directional 33.7% lift in recurring donor conversion. This experiment ran during a high urgency campaign and what we found was that the increase in recurring donor conversion was much higher at the beginning of the campaign

experiments.title: How adding security indicators on a giving form impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Would we be able to increase donor conversion by increasing perceived security on donation pages.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/07/2018 - 04/11/2018
We hypothesized that we might be able to increase donor conversion on FamilyLife’s donation pages if we increased the perceived security near the credit card section of the donation page. To do this, we developed a treatment that added a blue box around the credit card information, added an image of a lock in this same section of the form and said, “Your information is secured”.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running this experiment for over two months, we weren't able to validate it, but we did see directional lift in donor conversion of 7%. What this tells us is that there is a mental cost to people when making a donation and that when we can relieve some of that friction - in this case by visually telling them that their information is secure - that we can decrease friction and increase conversion on the page.

experiments.title: How adding value proposition to a donation page can affect donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Does adding value proposition on a donation page increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/29/2017 - 10/23/2017
FamilyLife was running a test on their main donation page that tested the value proposition in the body copy of the page.
experiments.keyLearnings: There was not a significant or valid lift in conversion from the updated value proposition, so this test will need to be run again when there is a lot of traffic continuously to the page to validate the learning.

experiments.title: How adding an incentive to a donation page affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Does adding an incentive to the donation page increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/21/2017 - 08/16/2017
FamilyLife was running a name acquisition offer that included a summer reading list for dads. They wanted to test if adding an incentive to the donation form would increase donor conversion- in this case, one of the books mentioned in the offer. All other elements of the page remained the same.
experiments.keyLearnings: While this experiment didn’t validate, we saw a trend towards a significant increase in donor conversion if the test had continued to run. This could be due to the perceived value of the incentive, and/or because it increased the value of the offer itself by giving readers a headstart on the summer reading list.

experiments.title: How the type of ask affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Does a direct ask or a soft ask in the call to action produce better donor conversion in a high urgency campaign email?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/24/2017 - 08/25/2017
FamilyLife was running a high urgency campaign and wanted to test a direct ask vs. a softer ask in the call to action of email #3 to see which motivated more people to click through to the donation page and give.
experiments.keyLearnings: This experiment didn’t validate, but we did see a trend towards higher donations from the soft ask version of the email. This is likely due to higher clickthrough rate which led to higher overall donations, but the same conversion rate.

experiments.title: How adding value proposition to a headline on a homepage banner affects donation conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Does a value prop-focused headline on a banner increase donations more than a match-focused headline on a banner?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/21/2017 - 12/27/2017
FamilyLife had a high-urgency banner focused on their 2 million dollar matching gift for their year-end campaign. They wanted to test whether a banner that led with value prop headline first instead of match language would increase donations more than a match-focused headline on banner.
experiments.keyLearnings: The test did not validate, likely because there was not enough difference in the variables tested against the control version to statistically affect conversion rate. The timing of the test was also during the end of their year-end campaign, when many people were already highly motivated to give once they got to the website.

experiments.title: How a radical redesign impacts donor conversion during year-end.
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a radical redesign motivate more people to convert?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/19/2018 - 01/27/2018
For FamilyLife’s year-end campaign, they had a special matching gift they offered to people in an effort to motivate more people to give during this critical fundraising campaign. The donation page was focused on this matching gift and encouraged people to give before December 31 to take advantage of this incentive. We hypothesized that while people will be motivated by this incentive, they might be more motivated if we increased urgency and reminded them of what their gift will be used for. We developed a radical redesign of the donation page that led with a FamilyLife value proposition, increased urgency with a countdown clock and also included the match incentive.
experiments.keyLearnings: The experiment didn’t validate, but we did see a directional lift in conversion of 10%. An incentive like a match is great, but we are able to motivate more people to convert if we can also remind them of what their gift is going towards and increase urgency visually. People don’t give to give, they give to have an impact and we must communicate what that impact is.
